other_observers: Mark Peterson SeEtta Moss after Mark had left; Brian Gibbons found the bird in the AM of the same day.
description: This was a shorebird about the same size or slightly smaller than a Lesser Yellowlegs and considerably smaller than Greater Yellowlegs (see photo) which it feed amongst.   When first seen (by me) the bird was far away but the strong buffy color of the breast stood out from a great distance. When Mark Peterson and I accidentally flushed the bird, I was suprised at how long the wings were. I knew to look for the U-shaped white uppertail coverts which I saw as it passed by in flight. Even at the far distance, > 100 meters, a droop to the bill tip was visible.  Luckily I got to see it again much closer and here is what I noticed while it was foraging and preening.  Head was buff colored with dark bill. The bill was thick based and there was a noticeable droop to its tip. The bill appeared to be slightly longer than the head as measure from back of the head to the base of the bill. At times the light feathering just behind the base of the bill was visible. The crown had noticable dark streaking on a buffy background. The face was buffy brown. The back was noticably scaly in appearance, like that seen in Buff-breasted Sandpiper. At times, I could see the birds scapulars stick up over the humped back. I have a photo of this but will only send if requested.  The legs were mostly not in view because of either water or vegetation but in the far view they appeared to be yellowish. I didn't get a chance to notice their length unfortunately.  The breast was unmarked and dark buffy. The hindneck was buffy brown with darker streaks.Behaviors: Fed mostly in dense Kochea (sp) and unknown sedge species, usually in areas slightly less deep than where both Greater and Lesser Yellowlegs fed. I never actually saw the bird probe into the water although it spent lots of its time searching the sedges.  No unusual behaviors noted.Call: none givenPlumage: juvenile
